## [00:00 - 03:02]

Hi, this is Don Crowther. Let's talk about a different way of doing webinars that has actually gotten some traction out there. And that is instead of doing a traditional webinar, it's actually doing live broadcast on Facebook live and YouTube live that are cells events. So, both of these sites, Facebook and YouTube, have large audiences, most of whom actually like them. And they have trained those people to actually do things like turn on notifications, which has the side effect of they get alerts when you go live. You go live. They've been working on training them to watch live broadcasts as their way of competing with the Netflix's of the world. And so Facebook and YouTube have a large vested interest in getting people to watch live broadcast. And so they give preferential treatment to them. And they do all kinds of things to promote them. And there are a lot of people who are starting to use that to their advantage. And so this raises a question. Should you, or will you ever just be doing your webinars on Facebook live or YouTube live? So I've seen a number of people do this fairly successfully. I've seen people in the information space in the software space, I've seen people selling blouses and shoes. I've seen them selling cultured pearls still in the shell. I've seen them selling event tickets, tickets to a concert or something like that. I have seen them selling services. All kinds of things are being sold using this methodology. So let's look at the characteristics of those who succeed in doing this. First off, they tend to have an audience that they primarily communicate with on that platform. Many of them don't even have the email addresses of those people. They just have this group of people who they primarily communicate with on that platform. Think of Beyonce or one of the other big celebrities who have Instagram lists and Facebook lists of the dozens and hundreds of millions of people. They don't have all those email addresses, I bet you they wish they did, but they don't. And so that's their primary means of communicating with them. Bridge that down to normal people. And you probably, if you've done social media work,

## [03:02 - 06:02]

have an audience on social media who only follows you on social media. They're not on your email list. They're no, they're no place else in connection with you. And so you're primarily communicating with them on that platform. Second off, those who succeed tend to regularly use stream video, Facebook live, YouTube live to communicate with them. And so people are used to seeing them there, seeing them present, seeing them talk and recognizing this is live TV and they're just going to be there and be human and transparent and real. Next, those who succeed have a substantial audience who follows them there and watches them there. And usually those who succeed in a big way have sold some stuff prior to this to that audience using that vehicle. And so that people are used to the fact that they may get some kind of offer from them on this particular service. So now let's just take this and illustrate this out in in like a model here of the promotional model. So it starts off by doing regular regular live streaming video that's not trying to sell anything, you're just regularly streaming video live to them. Now you can say, hey, go over here to look at my site or go over here to shop in my store, but they're not primarily sales oriented videos. They are communicating videos. Then over time, you begin to promote a special event. That special event, it becomes your sales event, where you're going to have a series of live streaming videos that are primarily designed to sell. After you're done with that event, then you return again to the regular non-sales live streaming videos. So let's look at these elements. Pre-launch, you've got that regular pattern of stream video usually face to camera, not screen capture. And then what you what you tend to do here is you start talking about the sales event days or weeks in event. Now you don't call it a sales event unless you're like doing a prime days or something like that. You're not talking about it as a sales event, you're talking about a special event. And then you turn that event into something special that people are really anticipating as it approaches. That specialness can be conveyed through, yes, discounts or this is the first time anyone's been able to get this, or it also can be this is a big giveaway, or I've got special guests coming on with me, or I've got special information, I'm only going to be communicating during this period of time. that this is going to be a very special time period, and they're going to want to be paying attention to it during that time. So now we get into the sales event. with this process. So I recommend on these broadcasts that you send them to a sales page so you don't have to explain everything about the product live. That's great because it enables you to keep this engagement relationship based cell going by letting your sales page do some of that work to do the heavy lifting of what it is that you're offering.
